name: Steel Production (Carburised Bloom to Crucible and Bessemer Lineages)
tagline: Controlling carbon in iron to balance hardness and toughness — the material behind machines
problem:
Bloomery iron is variable: soft in some regions, glassy in others, never quite the same batch to batch. Tools that must keep edges need higher carbon; structural beams need toughness. The steel problem is reproducible control of iron–carbon chemistry and microstructure: enough carbon to harden, not so much that blades chip, with processes to refine inclusions and adjust properties after forging.

Historically, multiple lineages converged: pack carburisation of wrought iron bars, crucible melting of blister steel, finery/puddling converting pig iron, and eventually Bessemer and open-hearth bulk decarburisation. The Codex unifies them under one entry because the user-facing outcome is the same material class — steel — even though industrial paths differ.

For civilisation restart, the earliest accessible path is often forge carburisation plus quench-and-temper heuristics on wrought iron, scaling toward crucible steel once refractories and temperature measurement improve.
overview:
Steel is iron alloyed with roughly 0.2–2.1% carbon in modern definitions of steels (above that enters cast iron territory, though historical language blurs). Carbon in interstitial solid solution and as iron carbide changes hardness and hardenability. Heat treatment — austenitising, quenching to form martensite, tempering to reduce brittleness — is as important as composition.

Pack carburisation packs iron bars in charcoal powder inside sealed clay boxes heated for hours to days; carbon diffuses inward producing a gradient — shear surfaces can be ground to high-carbon while core stays tough. Quenching in water or oil locks a hard surface.

Crucible steel (e.g., historical wootz/ulibar traditions) melts a selected charge in sealed crucibles, enabling more homogeneous alloy distribution before forging. This demands higher temperatures and excellent refractories.

Industrial revolution paths start from pig iron rich in carbon and silicon; oxygen or air oxidation lowers carbon under controlled conditions (Bessemer converter), or puddling stirs molten iron in a reverberatory furnace to expose it to oxidising slag. These are civilisation-scale technologies requiring large capital.

Regardless of path, slag inclusion control, phosphorus/sulphur limits, and consistent forging practice separate ornamental metal from reliable machine steel.

principles.name: Iron–carbon phase diagram
principles.explanation: Austenite can dissolve more carbon than ferrite; cooling paths determine pearlite, martensite, or graphite precipitation depending on rate and composition.

principles.name: Diffusion carburisation
principles.explanation: Carbon moves into solid iron over time and temperature; case depth scales with sqrt(time) approximately — thick cases need long soaks.

principles.name: Martensite formation
principles.explanation: Rapid quench traps carbon in a distorted lattice — very hard and brittle until tempered to allow carbide precipitation control.

principles.name: Hardenability and quench severity
principles.explanation: Alloy elements and section thickness determine whether a quench fully hardens the interior or only the surface.

components.name: Carburising pack or atmosphere furnace
components.criticalNote: Seals must exclude excess oxygen or you decarburise instead

components.name: Quench bath
components.criticalNote: Oils can ignite — fire protocol mandatory

components.name: Tempering hearth
components.function: Reheats hardened steel to trade hardness for toughness
components.position: Lower temperature zone than welding heat
components.madeFrom: Forge with temperature judgement or oven
components.criticalNote: Over-temper softens excessively — colour charts help historically

components.name: Crucible (advanced path)
components.criticalNote: Liquid steel dissolves refractories — contamination path

rawMaterials.name: Wrought iron or bloom stock
rawMaterials.purpose: Low-carbon starting matrix for carburisation or puddled iron for refining
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Forge consolidate before carburising to reduce slag stringers

rawMaterials.name: Alloying elements (optional)
rawMaterials.purpose: Improves hardenability, corrosion resistance (later stainless lineages)
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Imported pure elements in advanced industry
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Metallurgical knowledge to avoid embrittling phases

buildSteps.title: Normalise starting iron
buildSteps.description: Heat to bright orange and air cool to refine grain from prior forging. Test hardness with file — should cut easily if low carbon.

buildSteps.title: Pack carburise
buildSteps.description: Embed bars in charcoal fines with small carbonate addition if slag control is needed. Seal box, heat 850–950 °C range for many hours depending on case depth target.
buildSteps.warningNote: CO-rich atmosphere — outdoor furnace only with ventilation planning

buildSteps.title: Quench
buildSteps.description: Heat to non-magnetic point (Curie check with magnet) for simple carbon steels as a historic heuristic, then quench perpendicular entry to reduce distortion.
buildSteps.warningNote: Steam jacket can delay quench — agitate safely

buildSteps.title: Temper
buildSteps.description: Polish a face to observe oxide colours; temper until straw to blue depending on desired hardness for tool role.
buildSteps.warningNote: Uneven temper creates hard spots that chip

buildSteps.title: Test and iterate
buildSteps.description: Break sample coupons if possible; check grain with etched macroetch later when acids exist. Document heat colours and timings — this is your future standard.
buildSteps.warningNote: Snap tests of high-carbon pieces are sharp — gloves

history.event: Pattern-welded and carburised blades demonstrate empirical mastery of heterogeneous steel structures.
Benjamin Huntsman's crucible steel process in Sheffield improves homogeneity for tools.
Bessemer converter enables mass decarburisation of molten pig iron, launching cheap structural steel.

inventors.name: Henry Bessemer
inventors.contribution: Pneumatic steelmaking from molten pig iron

inventors.name: Benjamin Huntsman
inventors.contribution: Crucible melting for homogeneous tool steel

impact:
Steel is the skeleton of industrial machines: shafts, gears, rails, boilers, and engines. Reliable steel changes what is worth building — bridges expand, railways span continents, turbines spin.

For recovery, steelmaking is a knowledge bottleneck: you can have iron and still fail for decades without carbon control lore. This entry is the conceptual bridge from 'metal' to 'machine metal'.
